source.title: telemetry-guided pretriage sanity-checker and lightweight oncall hook
source.author: Maya Singh
source.release_notes: Add a lightweight telemetry-guided sanity checker and a pretriage CLI hook to reduce oncall noise; opt-in by default for slo-toolkit deployments; includes docs and telemetry metrics for tuning.
source.description: Motivation: on-call teams spend time investigating noisy alerts that are detectable as instrumentation anomalies (missing series, stale exporters, or thin traffic windows) before starting a full incident triage. This PR adds a lightweight 'pretriage sanity checker' that runs a small set of telemetry probes when an alert is received and surfaces a short human-readable verdict and artifacts to the responder. Design summary: implement a probe runner (timeout-first, non-blocking) that evaluates a configurable probe set against Prometheus and tracing backends, computes a compact fingerprint, and emits a JSON snapshot + human summary. Ship a CLI hook and a webhook payload for incident-bot/slo-toolkit to call as part of pretriage. Safety and opt-in: default checks are safe read-only Prometheus queries and a trace existence probe; probes and timeouts are configurable per-team via runtime config. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout to 10% of responses for two weeks, converge on rule tweaks, then enable org-wide. Checklist: 1) implement probe runner and schema; 2) add incident-bot hook + webhook contract; 3) surface results in oncall brief (summary line + artifact link); 4) add docs and runbook snippets; 5) add metrics and dashboard for false-positive rate. Testing: unit tests for probe parsing and mock Prom queries; integration test that exercises the webhook path using a local Prometheus test server; end-to-end smoke in canary environment. Related work: builds on prior compact-snapshot work and the 'telemetry-sanity-prober' spike (ENG-1982) and coordinates with incident-bot runbook suggestions. Backwards compatibility: webhook payloads are additive; incident-bot falls back if pretriage fails or times out. Implementation notes: the probe runner uses a 1.5s per-probe soft timeout and an overall 5s hard timeout; queries are sampled at scrubbed time windows for PII safety. Observability: new metrics prefixed slo.pretriage.* (attempts, timeouts, probe_failures, judgments) and a dashboard template in docs/dashboards/pretriage.md. Migration steps: operators can toggle feature via SLO_TOOLKIT_PRETRIAGE_ENABLED and tune PROBE_SET_PATH. Security: no credentials stored; Prometheus read-only credentials documented. This PR description is a compact summary of the commits, files changed, review thread, and CI outcome.
source.commits: a1f4b2c: add probe runner and probe schema; b5d2c7f: wire incident-bot webhook + CLI hook; c3e8fa9: add metrics + dashboard template; d7b1a44: docs: pretriage runbook and operator guide; e9c2d11: tests and flakiness fixes
review_comments: Sangeeta Rao: Please add a metric for 'probe_happy_path_duration' so we can alert on regressions — also verify the 5s hard timeout won't block high-throughput routes.
Maya Singh: Added histogram metric and verified non-blocking behavior; moved network calls into bounded goroutine pool; updated docs to recommend 5s only for low-volume routes.
Ethan Park: Minor nit on schema.md examples (use UTC timestamps), otherwise LGTM after CI passes.
Jonas Becker: Could we add an opt-out annotation for legacy services? Suggest adding SERVICE_PRETRIAGE=false support; I left a small patch in runner.go.
Maya Singh: Added opt-out annotation handling and unit test (tests/integration covers opt-out).
source.merge_commit_message: slo-toolkit: telemetry-guided pretriage sanity checker (adds probe runner, webhook, docs, metrics)
source.post_merge_actions: Deployed to canary namespace, feature flag enabled for 10% of alert webhooks. Dashboards created under org SLO -> Pretriage. Notified oncall and SE teams in #oncall-tooling.
source.ci_summary: All checks passed: unit (ok), integration (ok), lint (ok). One flaky e2e rerun required; reran and green. Coverage delta +0.8%.
source.notes_for_ops: Default probe set lives at /etc/slo-toolkit/pretriage/default_probes.yaml. To disable pretriage for a service, set SERVICE_PRETRIAGE=false in service annotations or toggle SLO_TOOLKIT_PRETRIAGE_ENABLED=false in deployment config.
source.examples_and_docs: docs/runbooks/pretriage-guide.md includes example webhook payloads and sample queries. The CLI 'slo-pretriage' supports --local-run to reproduce pretriage output locally.
source.testing_matrix: unit: go test ./...; integration: mocked Prom server (local CI); e2e: canary namespace run using incident-bot webhook emulator. Observability smoke verifies slo.pretriage.attempts > 0 after canary deployment.
source.estimated_impact: Expected to reduce mean triage time for noisy alerts by ~30% in the canary group; will monitor false-positive suppression rate and adjust probe thresholds.
source.author_acknowledgement: I confirm credentials are not stored and queries are scrubbed; ownership transferred to oncall-tooling for maintenance after merge.

title: telemetry-guided pretriage sanity-checker and lightweight oncall hook
preview: Motivation: on-call teams spend time investigating noisy alerts that are detectable as instrumentation anomalies (missing series, stale exporters, or thin traffic windows) before starting a full incident triage. This PR adds a lightweight 'pretriage sanity checker' that runs a small set of telemetry probes when an alert is received and surfaces a short human-readable verdict and artifacts to the responder. Design summary: implement a probe runner (timeout-first, non-blocking) that evaluates a configurable probe set against Prometheus and tracing backends, computes a compact fingerprint, and emits a JSON snapshot + human summary. Ship a CLI hook and a webhook payload for incident-bot/slo-toolkit to call as part of pretriage. Safety and opt-in: default checks are safe read-only Prometheus queries and a trace existence probe; probes and timeouts are configurable per-team via runtime config. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout to 10% of responses for two weeks, converge on rule tweaks, then enable org-wide. Checklist: 1) implement probe runner and schema; 2) add incident-bot hook + webhook contract; 3) surface results in oncall brief (summary line + artifact link); 4) add docs and runbook snippets; 5) add metrics and dashboard for false-positive rate. Testing: unit tests for probe parsing and mock Prom queries; integration test that exercises the webhook path using a local Prometheus test server; end-to-end smoke in canary environment. Related work: builds on prior compact-snapshot work and the 'telemetry-sanity-prober' spike (ENG-1982) and coordinates with incident-bot runbook suggestions. Backwards compatibility: webhook payloads are additive; incident-bot falls back if pretriage fails or times out. Implementation notes: the probe runner uses a 1.5s per-probe soft timeout and an overall 5s hard timeout; queries are sampled at scrubbed time windows for PII safety. Observability: new metrics prefixed slo.pretriage.* (attempts, timeouts, probe_failures, judgments) and a dashboard template in docs/dashboards/pretriage.md. Migration steps: operators can toggle feature via SLO_TOOLKIT_PRETRIAGE_ENABLED and tune PROBE_SET_PATH. Security: no credentials stored; Prometheus read-only credentials documented. This PR description is a compact summary of the commits, files changed, review thread, and CI outcome.

source.title: Add rollout-scoped burn-rate endpoint (route/model_version/region) with query caching
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description:
Summary: This adds first-class support in slo-toolkit for querying burn-rate scoped to a rollout slice (route + model_version + region + optional customer_tier). The rollout controller needs to evaluate fast/slow burn windows against the exact scope of the rollout step; previously we only supported coarse service-level burn-rate and a handful of static labels, which caused both false positives (baseline traffic in other regions) and false negatives (rollout-only regression diluted by global volume). This PR introduces (1) a new endpoint that accepts rollout scope and returns multi-window burn rate + sample sizes, (2) a query-planning layer to avoid N+1 PromQL fanout, and (3) an in-process cache + singleflight to reduce backend load and smooth bursty controller polling.

API changes: - New endpoint: GET /v1/burn-rate/rollout?service=<svc>&route=<route>&slo=<slo_name>&model_version=<ver>&region=<region>&customer_tier=<tier>&windows=5m,30m (customer_tier optional; windows optional, default 5m,30m). - Existing endpoint /v1/burn-rate remains unchanged. - Response includes: burn_rate_by_window, error_budget_remaining, request_count_by_window, evaluation_timestamp, data_freshness_seconds, and query_fingerprint. Note: response schema is still JSON but this PR keeps it backward compatible by only adding fields to the rollout endpoint.

Implementation details: - Scope normalization: route/model_version/region/customer_tier are normalized and validated against an allowlist to avoid unbounded label injection. We reject scopes that contain regex meta characters, wildcard operators, or exceed length limits (route 128, model_version 64, region 32, tier 32). - Query planning: we build a single PromQL expression per window that computes both burn rate and request volume for the same scope, rather than issuing separate queries. This reduces total remote_read calls from 4->2 per evaluation (two windows). - Cache: adds an LRU+TTL cache keyed by (service, route, slo, model_version, region, tier, windows, time_bucket). TTL defaults to 15s; time_bucket floors to 10s to improve hit rate during controller polling. Uses singleflight to coalesce concurrent identical queries (common during step transitions). Cache entries store both result and remote error to prevent thundering herds during backend partial outages (short negative caching, 5s). - Staleness handling: surfaces data_freshness_seconds computed from the newest sample timestamp returned by the query; if freshness > 120s we return 200 but mark stale=true in headers (X-SLO-Data-Stale: true) and include freshness in the payload so the controller can treat it as “monitor stale” and suppress auto-actions per policy.

Performance impact: On staging load tests simulating 1k rollout evaluations/min across 50 scopes, remote query QPS dropped ~62% due to caching + singleflight. p95 endpoint latency improved from 420ms -> 180ms with the same backend.

Security/abuse considerations: Added strict input validation, label allowlist, max windows count=4, and max time range=1h. Also added basic rate-limit hooks (existing middleware) specifically for the rollout endpoint to avoid accidental runaway loops.

Testing: - Unit tests for scope validation, key normalization, and cache behavior (positive + negative caching). - Integration test with the fake prom backend ensures query composition stays stable and returns expected sample counts. - Added benchmark for cache hit/miss path and singleflight concurrency.

Rollout plan: - This is gated behind feature flag in the rollout controller (tracked in redwood PR-28458 / PR-28463). - Observability-pack dashboards/alerts will use the same query fingerprint to correlate evaluation events with backend queries.

Refs: Linear ENG-4826; depends on rollout controller wiring in redwood; aligns with ADR-0127 (dual-window burn-rate + min-sample gating) by returning both fast/slow windows and request volume in one response.
commits: api: add /v1/burn-rate/rollout handler and request parsing
query: implement rollout scope query builder and combined burn-rate+volume promql
cache: add lru+ttl cache with singleflight coalescing
validation: enforce label allowlist and length/character constraints
tests: add unit + integration coverage and concurrency test for singleflight
bench: add microbench for cache hit rate and handler overhead
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Can we be explicit about cardinality guardrails here? Adding model_version/route in queries can explode quickly. Aisha Bello: Yep—added allowlist + max length + disallow regex operators. Also capping windows and range. Pushed commit 9f3c2b1.

Logan Wright: Cache TTL at 30s might mask regressions during fast rollouts. Can we tighten? Aisha Bello: Agree. Dropped TTL to 15s and added 10s time bucketing for hit rate; controller will still poll every ~10s. Also added data freshness surfacing.

Jordan Lee: Need request_count_by_window in response for min-sample gating (controller side). Aisha Bello: Added request_count_by_window and query_fingerprint so controller can log/trace evaluation decisions to the backend query.

Rafael Mendes: What do we do if backend is partially down? Should we fail closed? Aisha Bello: Endpoint returns 502 on remote query failure (same as existing), but we add short negative caching to prevent stampede. Controller policy decides suppress vs pause on monitor missing/stale; staleness is returned as freshness seconds.

Approved-by: Allison Grant, Logan Wright. Final note: Please ensure we emit cache hit/miss metrics. Aisha Bello: Added slo_toolkit_cache_hits_total{cache="burn_rate_rollout"} and misses_total with status labels.
source.release_notes:
- slo-toolkit: new rollout-scoped burn-rate endpoint for route/model_version/region queries
- Includes in-process caching + singleflight to reduce backend query load
- Returns request volume + freshness to support debounce/min-sample logic in rollout controller

source.title: Add incident remediation SLA policy evaluator (due times + breach classification)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Adds a small library + CLI for computing remediation SLA due timestamps and evaluating breach status based on incident severity, tier, and incident_type/subtype. Intended to be called by incident-bot and alerting pipelines; no customer-facing changes.
source.description: Context: As part of the Reliability Program (Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping), we need a single, audited implementation for remediation SLA timers. Today, multiple systems (incident-bot, dashboards, ad-hoc scripts) are calculating “SLA due” inconsistently, and edge cases (pause/override, force majeure, customer-caused) are handled differently. This PR introduces a small, dependency-light library in slo-toolkit to: (1) compute due_at for remediation SLAs from incident metadata, (2) evaluate whether an incident is currently in breach / at risk, and (3) provide a stable, versioned policy surface we can share across services. Scope / Approach: - Adds @redwood/sla-policy (library) with a compact policy model that takes severity (Sev0–Sev3), tier (Hosted | Dedicated | Private), and taxonomy (incident_type + optional subtype) and returns: time_to_mitigate_minutes, time_to_fix_minutes, due_mitigate_at, due_fix_at, and breach status given timestamps. - Includes a CLI tool `sla-policy` for debugging and validation (used by SRE during rollout + backfills). - Policy is defined as a checked-in JSON allowlist (low-cardinality, v1 taxonomy only) to avoid runtime lookups and to ensure “unknown/unclassified” behavior is explicit. - Supports pause windows and overrides via an optional `adjustments` array (e.g., pause from incident_start->provider_outage_resolved). These are treated as auditable inputs; the evaluator does not infer pauses. - Adds explicit handling for: unknown incident_type, missing tier, and “customer-caused” incidents (requires an explicit flag; otherwise default policy applies). Key API: - `computeSla(incident, policy, now)` returns `SlaResult`. - `evaluateBreach(slaResult, now)` returns `ok | at_risk | breached` with remaining_minutes. - `validatePolicy(policy)` is run at startup/CLI and in CI to prevent unbounded growth and to ensure all keys match the v1 taxonomy allowlist. Example: `const r = computeSla({ incident_id: "INC-24891", started_at: "2026-02-02T19:22:00Z", severity: "SEV1", tier: "DEDICATED", incident_type: "capacity", subtype: "regional_gpu_shortage" }, defaultPolicy, new Date());` returns due timestamps aligned with the remediation SLA matrix, and then `evaluateBreach` provides `at_risk` vs `breached` given the current time. Notes / Alignment: - Policy values and key names align with Confluence “Owner mapping + remediation SLA matrix” and the incident-bot required taxonomy fields rollout. - This evaluator is used by downstream work (redwood PR-28635 breach alerting + incident-bot gating) to ensure consistent SLA timers across systems. Testing: - Unit tests cover due time computation, pause/override windows, unknown keys defaulting behavior, and a set of golden test vectors derived from 8 real incidents (anonymized) from the last 90 days backfill. - Adds a policy validation test that fails CI if new policy keys are added without updating the allowlist + docs. Rollout plan: - Land library first; consumers will vendor it via semver tag from slo-toolkit. - SRE will run CLI against a sample of recent incidents to verify due_at matches the matrix before enabling breach paging. - Follow-up will wire incident-bot to show due_at and “time remaining” in the incident channel header.
commits: feat(sla-policy): add policy model + TypeScript types
feat(sla-policy): implement computeSla() and evaluateBreach()
feat(cli): add sla-policy CLI (compute + validate + explain)
test(sla-policy): add golden vectors + pause/override coverage
chore(ci): run policy validation in PR checks
docs: add usage examples + integration notes for incident-bot/alerts
review_conversation: Allison Grant: Can we confirm this won’t introduce metrics label cardinality issues? We should be careful about letting arbitrary incident_type strings into the evaluator output.

Aisha Bello: Good call. The evaluator only accepts incident_type/subtype that match the taxonomy allowlist; otherwise it returns `UNKNOWN` and uses the explicit default SLA. Also, `explain` output in the CLI is not intended for metrics labels—consumers should label only with the canonical incident_type enum (which already matches the allowlist). I added a test that ensures unknown keys don’t get echoed back as “normalized” labels.
Rafael Mendes: Two questions: (1) For Dedicated Sev1, do we distinguish mitigate vs fix? (2) What’s the behavior if started_at is missing (created_at vs declared_at differences)?

Aisha Bello: (1) Yes—policy returns both time_to_mitigate and time_to_fix. Consumers can alert on either; incident-bot will display both. (2) If started_at is missing we fail validation (throws) by default; there’s an optional `fallback_started_at` knob in computeSla() for ingestion pipelines that only have created_at. I added docs + tests and kept the default strict so we don’t silently shift timers.
Devon Price: For incident-bot integration we need a stable JSON output and a single command to compute due_at. Can the CLI output machine-readable JSON without extra text?
Aisha Bello: Added `--json` flag to `sla-policy compute` that emits a single JSON line (no surrounding logs). Also added `--quiet` to suppress validation warnings when running in bots.
Dylan Brooks: Looks good. Minor: please align severity naming with incident-bot (“SEV0” not “P0”). Also, can we add a short note about pause windows needing an audit trail?

Aisha Bello: Updated the enum names to SEV0–SEV3 and added a README note: pause/override inputs must come from an auditable source (incident timeline annotations or approved overrides), evaluator stays pure/deterministic.
Allison Grant: Approving. Thanks for adding the allowlist guardrail.
Rafael Mendes: Approved. Please link this in ENG-4829 so breach alerting consumes the same library.
Dylan Brooks: Approved.

source.title: CLI: generate tiered SLO configs + validate metric coverage by tier/endpoint/region
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: This PR adds CLI tooling to (1) generate tier-aware SLO config files from a canonical targets table and (2) validate metric coverage required to compute SLIs per tier/endpoint/region. This is intended to unblock the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program by making SLO config generation repeatable and preventing silent SLO data gaps (e.g., missing tier tags at ingest, missing streaming disconnect counters, or latency histograms not exported in a region). The generator produces configs compatible with the redwood SLO loader/schema introduced in redwood/pr-28438 (same field names and tier labeling). The validator checks that required metrics exist and are queryable for each config row, with guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions (no per-customer/per-model checks by default; validation is done at metric+label-key level). The CLI is intended for CI usage (fail fast) and for on-call debugging (show what’s missing + suggested instrumentation owners).
source.summary_of_changes: Added `sloctl` subcommands: `generate` (targets -> config) and `validate-metric-coverage` (config -> coverage report + exit code). Implemented a small metric catalog describing required series for availability, latency, and streaming reliability SLIs, including label-key expectations for `tier`, `region`, and `endpoint_class`. Added a Prometheus-compatible query backend (default) with a lightweight adapter interface so we can plug in Mimir later. Added CI-friendly outputs: JSON report and a human-readable table. Added docs and examples for Hosted vs Dedicated tiers and region scoping. Added unit tests for config generation, metric requirements expansion, and validation semantics (including exclusion cases like rate-limited requests).
source.user_facing_usage: Examples: `sloctl generate --targets ./data/tiered_slo_targets.csv --out ./configs/tiered_slos.yaml --default-window 30d` ; `sloctl validate-metric-coverage --config ./configs/tiered_slos.yaml --prom-url $PROM_URL --regions us-east-1,us-west-2,eu-west-1 --tier hosted --fail-on missing_required --format table` ; `sloctl validate-metric-coverage --config ./configs/tiered_slos.yaml --prom-url $PROM_URL --format json --out ./artifacts/slo_metric_coverage.json`
source.notes_on_validation_semantics: Validation checks for metric existence by running low-cost label discovery queries (e.g., `count by(__name__)(...)` / `label_values`) over a short lookback window (default 15m; configurable). It verifies: required metric names exist in the region(s); required label keys exist on the series (`tier`, `region`, and `endpoint_class`); and for histogram-based latency SLIs, that `_bucket`, `_sum`, `_count` series are present. It does NOT validate per-label-value cardinality by default (no `tier=dedicated` value checks unless `--check-label-values` is explicitly set), to avoid false negatives during partial rollout or sparse traffic. Streaming reliability validation expects either `api_stream_disconnect_total` (preferred) or the legacy `api_stream_ended_total{reason=...}` mapping; the report calls out which path is detected and recommends the preferred metric if only legacy is found.
source.generated_config_shape: Generated config output matches the redwood SLO schema expectations: name (stable), tier (hosted|dedicated), endpoint_class (chat|completions|embeddings|rerank|streaming), region (explicit), window (rolling, default 30d), slis (availability, latency, streaming where applicable), and exclusions (invalid_request, rate_limited, client_abort). The generator supports per-tier defaults and per-endpoint overrides (e.g., stricter latency targets for Dedicated chat in us-east-1 vs Hosted).
source.security_and_access_notes: The validator requires read-only access to the metrics backend. No customer identifiers are queried; queries are aggregated and scoped to region/metric/label keys. A `--dry-run` mode prints the planned queries for review. The CLI redacts Prom URLs containing credentials in logs and supports `--prom-bearer-token-file` to avoid env var leakage in CI logs.
commits: feat(cli): add sloctl generate + validate-metric-coverage commands
feat(validation): metric catalog for tiered SLIs (availability/latency/streaming)
feat(backends): Prometheus query adapter with timeouts + retries
test: generator golden tests + validation unit tests
docs: add tiered SLO config generation + CI integration examples
chore: wire coverage report artifacts for GitHub Actions
source.testing: Unit: `go test ./...` ; Manual: validated against staging Prom for us-east-1/us-west-2 using a staged tiered config; verified detection of missing `tier` label on gateway request counters in eu-west-1 (expected failure), then re-ran with `--check-label-values=false` to confirm label-key-only behavior. Verified JSON output is stable for CI artifact diffing.
source.ci_checks: github-actions / build (go1.22): pass; github-actions / unit-tests: pass; github-actions / lint (golangci-lint): pass; github-actions / docs-links: pass
review_conversation: Allison Grant: Can we make sure we’re validating label *keys* not values by default? During rollout, Dedicated traffic in some regions will be sparse and this could flap CI. Also please document the lookback default and why.

Aisha Bello (author): Yep—default is label-key existence only. I added `--check-label-values` as opt-in (defaults false) and documented lookback defaults (15m) with rationale + override flag `--lookback`.

Thomas Baird: I’m worried about `label_values()` on high-cardinality metrics. Are we running it on anything with per-model labels? We should avoid accidentally pulling label sets on `model` or `customer_id` even if present.

Aisha Bello (author): Good call. Catalog explicitly scopes to metrics that are aggregated at gateway/routing/service level and we only request label values for a small allowlist of keys (`tier`, `region`, `endpoint_class`). Added a guard that errors if a query attempts to list non-allowlisted labels unless `--unsafe-allow-any-label` is provided (not for CI).

Logan Wright: Please align naming with the schema loader PR (redwood/pr-28438). Specifically, make sure endpoint_class values match what the loader expects and that the generator outputs stable `name` fields (no timestamps).

Aisha Bello (author): Updated endpoint_class enum to match loader (`chat`, `completions`, `embeddings`, `rerank`, `streaming`) and changed name generation to a deterministic slug: `{tier}.{region}.{endpoint_class}.{sli_set}`. Added a test that name stability holds across runs.
Jordan Lee: For the CI use-case, can we make the exit codes precise? I want to be able to gate on missing metrics vs temporary query errors/timeouts.

Aisha Bello (author): Added exit code mapping: 0 ok, 2 missing_required, 3 query_error, 4 invalid_config. Also added `--fail-on` policy with values `missing_required` (default) and `any` for strict mode.
Allison Grant: Approved after docs + guardrails updates.
Logan Wright: LGTM. This unblocks the rollout checklist item for metric coverage validation.
source.rollout_plan: Land CLI + docs. Next: wire `sloctl generate` and `sloctl validate-metric-coverage` into the redwood monorepo pipeline that owns SLO config artifacts (tracked in ENG-4926). Initial CI usage should run in warn-only mode (upload report artifact, do not fail) for 1 week, then move to fail-on missing_required once tier tagging at ingress is fully rolled out (redwood/pr-28411).
source.release_notes: Internal tooling: new `sloctl` commands for generating tiered SLO configs and validating metric coverage in Prometheus-compatible backends.

source.title: Add error budget burn-rate alerts for enterprise protected-route SLOs
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Adds standardized multi-window multi-burn-rate alert rules and recommended thresholds for enterprise protected-route SLOs (chat/embeddings/rerank). Includes low-cardinality label guidance and per-route overrides via route_group mapping.
source.body:
## Context
This PR adds burn-rate alerting presets to `slo-toolkit` for Redwood Hosted API enterprise protected routes as part of the Hot-route capacity protection initiative. The intent is to page on *fast* error-budget consumption for enterprise SLOs (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, embeddings, rerank) without generating paging noise during expected best-effort shedding.

Motivation:
- Prior incident and game-day findings showed we need early, reliable paging on *enterprise* SLO risk rather than generic fleet-wide latency/5xx signals.
- With tier-aware throttling/admission control rolling out, we expect 429s on best-effort; burn alerts should focus on enterprise SLI outcomes.

Links:
- Linear: ENG-4926 (Dashboards/alerts for protected routes), PM-882 (Enterprise SLO policy)
- Related PRs (for context): redwood/pr-27441 (429 reason codes), observability-pack/pr-1832 (dashboards)

## What this changes
### 1) New alert presets: enterprise protected routes
Adds new SLO alert rule templates for enterprise route SLOs using multi-window, multi-burn-rate (MWMB) patterns.

Implemented presets:
- `enterprise_protected_route_availability_burn` (availability SLOs)
- `enterprise_protected_route_latency_burn` (latency SLOs)

Windows/burn multipliers (defaults; can be overridden per SLO):
- Page (critical): 5m @ 14x AND 1h @ 14x (fast burn), plus 30m @ 6x AND 6h @ 6x (sustained burn)
- Ticket (warning): 2h @ 3x AND 1d @ 3x

Rationale for numbers:
- Mirrors common SRE MWMB guidance while being tuned for Redwood’s “protected routes” where a small amount of budget burn is meaningful.
- Avoids paging on short spikes that do not materially threaten monthly budget.

### 2) Labeling + routing guidance to keep cardinality bounded
Adds explicit guidance and guardrails in rule generation:
- Requires `route_group` rather than raw `path` labels for alert grouping.
- Allows `model_family` only where it’s a small bounded set; discourages `tenant_id`/`api_key` labels.

### 3) Per-route overrides without copy/paste
Introduces a small override mechanism:
- Defaults live in one place (enterprise protected route presets)
- Individual SLO definitions may override thresholds/windows via `alerting.overrides.*` fields in the SLO spec

### 4) Documentation updates
Adds a short doc page describing:
- When to use enterprise protected route burn alerts
- Recommended paging policy and ownership (SRE vs Platform on-call)
- Example config for chat/embeddings/rerank

## Example
Added an example SLO snippet:
- `route_group: chat_completions`
- `tier: enterprise`
- Applies the new burn presets and routes to `pagerduty_service: hosted-api-enterprise`

## Testing
- Unit tests for rule rendering (ensures correct PromQL shape, annotation fields, and routing labels)
- Golden tests updated for newly generated alert rules
- Lint (yaml/json schema) + CI pass

## Rollout / operational notes
- This PR does not enable alerts by itself; it updates toolkit templates consumed by the Observability Pack.
- Rollout should be coordinated with observability-pack/pr-1832 and the hosted API SLO definitions doc so we don’t page on incomplete SLI wiring.

## Checklist
- [x] Added presets + unit tests
- [x] Added docs and example SLO definition
- [x] Ensured label guidance avoids high-cardinality tags
- [x] Confirmed alert naming aligns with observability standards

---
## Changes (high-level)
- Add new enterprise protected-route burn-rate presets (availability + latency)
- Add override mechanism for windows/thresholds
- Add docs + example
- Update golden test fixtures

## Commits
1) slo-toolkit: add enterprise protected route burn presets
2) slo-toolkit: add override plumbing and schema validation
3) slo-toolkit: docs + examples
4) tests: update golden fixtures and add render tests

## Review notes / discussion summary
- Sergio: Requested that the 5m/1h critical windows be gated by a sustained pair to reduce paging on short-lived deploy blips. Implemented MWMB pairs and added runbook link placeholder in annotations.
- Allison: Flagged risk of `path` label cardinality. Updated templates to require `route_group` and added validation that rejects raw `path` for this preset.
- Logan: Asked to ensure we don’t treat 429s as failures for enterprise SLOs unless explicitly configured. Added doc note: enterprise SLI should count successful responses; 429s should be separated via reason codes and not mixed into error budget unless the SLO definition chooses to.
- Rafael: Confirmed thresholds match game-day outcomes; requested annotation fields include route_group/tier to make incident-bot messages readable. Added annotations and standardized labels.

## CI
Checks:
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- golden-tests: pass
- lint/schema: pass

Merge:
- Squash merged into `main` after approvals and green CI.
conversation: Sergio Costa (review): The 5m@14x is fine, but can we gate it with a longer window pair to avoid paging on brief deploy blips? Also please include runbook link in annotations if possible.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated to MWMB pairs: (5m,1h) and (30m,6h) for critical; added annotations for `route_group`, `tier`, and `runbook` (left as placeholder string consumed by downstream pack). Pushed commit 9c3f2a1.
Allison Grant (review): Please do not allow raw `path` as a grouping label for this preset. We’ll regret the cardinality. Route group only + bounded model family if needed.

Aisha Bello (author): Agreed. Added validation: enterprise protected-route presets require `route_group`; schema rejects `path` for this preset. Updated docs with label guidance. Pushed commit 2b71d5e.

Logan Wright (review): Make sure 429s don’t accidentally count as SLO failures for enterprise. We expect best-effort shedding; enterprise should be protected, but if enterprise sees 429-overload we’ll track separately.

Aisha Bello (author): Added doc note + example SLI query guidance (exclude 429 by default; track via separate overload metrics). No changes to SLI here since this toolkit only renders alerts from SLO spec, but clarified recommended setup.
Rafael Mendes (review): Looks good. Please ensure naming matches dashboard spec and that annotations include tier/route group for incident-bot readability.
Aisha Bello (author): Done; standardized alert name prefix `EnterpriseProtectedRoute` and added labels/annotations. Ready for re-review.
Sergio Costa (approve): Approved. MWMB approach + label restrictions are what we need.
Allison Grant (approve): Approved after schema validation change.
Rafael Mendes (approve): Approved. Let’s coordinate enablement with observability-pack.

source.title: oncall-memory-journal-and-telemetry-coalescer
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Motivation: On-call responders frequently need quick access to recent human decisions, triage notes, and distilled telemetry trends. This PR introduces a lightweight oncall-memory journal plus a telemetry coalescer to reduce noisy signal volume and surface concise, actionable summaries to the incident-bot and slo-toolkit dashboards.

What this change does (single-string description): Adds an append-only oncall memory service (daemon + small embedded store) that journals short annotations from responders and automated synthesis hooks; provides a compact HTTP API (/v1/memory/append, /v1/memory/query) and a CLI (slo-memory export --range) for exporting session summaries. Implements a telemetry coalescer that consumes existing telemetry streams (metrics/traces/log-keys) and produces pre-aggregated "event shards" using configurable fingerprinting rules (by service, alert-key, tag-set) and time-windowed coalescing (default 20s). The coalescer exposes a lightweight local cache used by the console and incident-bot to render condensed trendlines and sampled exemplars. Privacy and safety: the memory journal applies a redaction pipeline (PII regexes + allowlist for tokens) and emits audit events to the existing audit-log exporter.

Implementation notes: The memory daemon is implemented in Go, embedded BoltDB-backed append journal with a capped retention policy (configurable: default 72h). The daemon accepts JSON payloads limited to 1KB per entry and indexes by incident_id, responder_id, and source. The coalescer is implemented as a small Rust service (for low-latency memcoalescing) that subscribes to the metrics bus, applies coalescing heuristics defined in YAML, and writes rollups to Redis for fast reads. We've added a new adapter in incident-bot that will call the memory API when a responder posts a quick note or marks a step as 'confirmed'.

Backwards compatibility: services not using the new adapter see no change. Memory endpoints are behind a feature flag (slo_memory.enabled). Coalescer can run in a passive mode that doesn't write rollups for clusters that prefer external aggregation.

Testing and safety: unit tests for the redaction pipeline and coalescing heuristics included, integration tests for the CLI export and incident-bot adapter added. Benchmarks show <2ms median append latency and coalescer ingestion sustaining 10k events/s on the configured test harness.

Docs and migration: Added docs/operations/oncall-memory.md and a short quickstart in docs/cli/README.md. Migration notes outline a two-week opt-in pilot and an emergency purge command for compliance.

Why this is different: unlike previous snapshot exporters, this focuses on low-latency, small-footprint oncall memory with explicit privacy controls and a telemetry coalescer tuned for on-call consumption (short windows, exemplar preservation).
commits: a1f3b2c chore: add memory daemon scaffold and CLI
b2d4e6f feat: implement append/query endpoints and retention worker
c3e5f7a feat: telemetry coalescer initial ingestion + Redis rollups
d4f6a8b fix: redaction edge-case and unit-test coverage
e5a7b9c docs: add oncall-memory operator guide and quickstart
f6b8c0d ci: wire up integration tests and benchmark job
review_comments: Diego Alvarez: Nice approach. Two asks: (1) can the retention be set to 'per-tenant' in config? (2) please add a small benchmark in perf/ comparing coalescer modes.
Maya Chen (author): Retention is tenant-configurable now — added TTLPerTenant to the config and a migration doc. Added perf comparison in perf/coalescer/README.md as requested.
Priya Nair: We should avoid synchronous writes from the incident-bot path — does adapter buffer?
Maya Chen (author): Adapter now buffers in-memory and writes to memory daemon asynchronously; added circuit-breaker and fallback to ephemeral incident annotation when daemon is unreachable.
Ethan Park: Redaction regex looks good but add a small fuzz test for unicode edge-cases.
Maya Chen (author): Added fuzz test and increased redaction coverage in internal/memory/redaction_test.go
ci_checks: ci/integration-memory: pass (runnable in CI runner, containers mocked)
ci/bench: flaky on first run, rerun succeeded
release_notes: Add: oncall-memory service (append/query API) with retention and redaction
Add: telemetry coalescer producing short-window rollups and exemplars
Change: incident-bot adapter can push quick notes to oncall memory (opt-in)

This PR introduces a lightweight oncall-memory journal plus a telemetry coalescer to reduce noisy signal volume and surface concise, actionable summaries to the incident-bot and slo-toolkit dashboards.

What this change does (single-string description): Adds an append-only oncall memory service (daemon + small embedded store) that journals short annotations from responders and automated synthesis hooks; provides a compact HTTP API (/v1/memory/append, /v1/memory/query) and a CLI (slo-memory export --range) for exporting session summaries. Implements a telemetry coalescer that consumes existing telemetry streams (metrics/traces/log-keys) and produces pre-aggregated "event shards" using configurable fingerprinting rules (by service, alert-key, tag-set) and time-windowed coalescing (default 20s). The coalescer exposes a lightweight local cache used by the console and incident-bot to render condensed trendlines and sampled exemplars. Privacy and safety: the memory journal applies a redaction pipeline (PII regexes + allowlist for tokens) and emits audit events to the existing audit-log exporter.

Implementation notes: The memory daemon is implemented in Go, embedded BoltDB-backed append journal with a capped retention policy (configurable: default 72h). The daemon accepts JSON payloads limited to 1KB per entry and indexes by incident_id, responder_id, and source. The coalescer is implemented as a small Rust service (for low-latency memcoalescing) that subscribes to the metrics bus, applies coalescing heuristics defined in YAML, and writes rollups to Redis for fast reads. We've added a new adapter in incident-bot that will call the memory API when a responder posts a quick note or marks a step as 'confirmed'.

Backwards compatibility: services not using the new adapter see no change. Memory endpoints are behind a feature flag (slo_memory.enabled). Coalescer can run in a passive mode that doesn't write rollups for clusters that prefer external aggregation.

Testing and safety: unit tests for the redaction pipeline and coalescing heuristics included, integration tests for the CLI export and incident-bot adapter added. Benchmarks show <2ms median append latency and coalescer ingestion sustaining 10k events/s on the configured test harness.

Docs and migration: Added docs/operations/oncall-memory.md and a short quickstart in docs/cli/README.md. Migration notes outline a two-week opt-in pilot and an emergency purge command for compliance.

Why this is different: unlike previous snapshot exporters, this focuses on low-latency, small-footprint oncall memory with explicit privacy controls and a telemetry coalescer tuned for on-call consumption (short windows, exemplar preservation).

source.title: Add rollback gate rule library (error-rate + p95 latency) with defaults + overrides
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description:
Context: As part of Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs, we need a consistent way to evaluate rollout gates across services/environments. Prior implementations hard-coded thresholds and metric queries per rollout controller, which made it difficult to tune defaults, apply consistent tagging requirements (tenant/model/region/config_version), and explain gate decisions in the Console/runbooks.

This PR adds a small, reusable “gate rule library” to slo-toolkit with two initial rules used by the canary rollout controller:
- Error rate (5xx) gate
- p95 latency gate

Key points:
- Sane defaults aimed at Hosted/Dedicated canaries, with explicit override hooks for Dedicated/private installs.
- A single evaluation result object that includes a user-facing explanation string, the metric query/selector used, the time window, and the exact threshold evaluated.
- Strict tag requirements (tenant, model, region, config_version) with a “fail closed” option for production rollouts (default), and “warn only” for staging.
- Guardrails to reduce false positives: minimum request volume check + burn-in period support.

The rollout controller (redwood repo) will consume these rules in a follow-up PR; this PR is scoped to slo-toolkit only.

Testing:
- Added unit tests covering: threshold parsing, minimum sample gating, tag validation, override precedence, and explanation formatting.
- Added golden tests for rendered queries to make refactors safer.

Rollout:
- No behavior change until the rollout controller switches to these rules.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4827 (SLO gates: latency and 5xx), ENG-4824 (canary rollout controller)
- Related: perf-canary gate signals and dashboard tagging work (config_version annotations).
commits: feat(gates): add GateRule interface + evaluation result schema
feat(gates): implement ErrorRate5xxGate with defaults + overrides
feat(gates): implement P95LatencyGate with minimum volume + burn-in
feat(metrics): add tag validation helpers + config_version enforcement
test(gates): unit + golden tests for rule evaluation + query rendering
docs: add README section on configuring gate overrides
source.illustrative_snippet:
Example (p95 latency):
- Default threshold: +15% over baseline OR absolute > 900ms (whichever is stricter), evaluated over 10m window after 3m burn-in.
- Minimum volume: 2,000 requests per scope.
- Required tags: tenant, model, region, config_version.

Returned evaluation (abridged):
status=FAIL
reason="p95 latency 1.14s > 900ms (window=10m, scope=tenant=acme model=llama-3-70b region=us-east-1 config_version=ocv_1736)"
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): "Love the explanation string in the result — will help Console. Two asks: (1) ensure we always include the rendered selector with all required tags, and (2) add a distinct status for 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA' so controllers can pause rather than auto-fail in staging."
Aisha Bello (author): "Good call. I added `rendered_selector` to the result and introduced `INSUFFICIENT_DATA` with env policy (prod=fail-closed, staging=warn/pause). Added tests."
Sean Gallagher (review): "Can we make min-volume configurable per rule? Dedicated customers in low-traffic regions will otherwise get stuck."
Aisha Bello (author): "Yep — `min_samples` is now part of the override schema per gate. Default stays 2000 for Hosted canaries. README updated."
Benji Okafor (review): "From control plane side: please keep override precedence deterministic. We’ll likely pass overrides at rollout creation time and also have an env-level default."
Aisha Bello (author): "Implemented precedence: request_override > scope_override > env_default > rule_default. Added a unit test for precedence and documented it."
Allison Grant (approve): "Looks good now. The golden tests for query rendering are a nice touch."
Sean Gallagher (approve): "Ship it. Please follow up with a short runbook note on how to choose min volume for Dedicated."
Aisha Bello (author): "Ack — will add to the change-management policy/runbook in redwood docs."

source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Sean Gallagher.
release_notes: New: reusable SLO gate rule library with standard result/explanation format.
New: built-in error-rate (5xx) and p95 latency gates with safe defaults and override schema.
New: required metrics tags enforcement (tenant/model/region/config_version) with configurable fail-closed policy.

source.title: Add per-model performance SLO templates (p95 latency + tokens/sec) for perf-canary + rollout gates
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: This PR introduces first-class SLO Toolkit templates for per-model performance signals that we can reuse across regions/tier pools and plug into perf-canary alerting + rollout gates. We currently have ad-hoc Grafana + PromQL alerts in observability-pack that are hard to keep consistent, and they don't encode our recommended dampening/min-samples patterns. These templates are intended to standardize how we express and reason about performance regressions (latency and throughput) without turning performance into a classical "error budget" SLO. Scope: (1) Add new template families: ModelLatencyRegression and ModelThroughputRegression. (2) Provide guidance in-template for threshold selection (per model + SKU + region), minimum sample size, and dampening windows. (3) Add a small helper to keep label cardinality safe (standard label allowlist + enforced aggregation). (4) Add example configs for perf-canary Phase 1 (us-east) so SRE + Runtime can validate before enabling paging. Definitions: Latency regression signal = observed p95 end-to-end latency compared to a stored baseline p95 for the same model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hw_sku (+ optional region) for the same prompt-shape profile; regression computed as percent change and gated by minimum sample size over a rolling evaluation window. Throughput regression signal = observed tokens/sec (decode-dominant) compared to baseline tokens/sec for same dimensions; computed similarly. Templates intentionally avoid customer prompt data by only consuming perf-canary synthetic traffic metrics and baseline artifacts. Guidance included (docs) covers: recommended initial thresholds (warn at +10% p95 latency or -10% toks/sec; page at +20% or -20% sustained), how to tune per model based on variance, when to split thresholds by region/SKU, and how to avoid alert storms due to baseline drift or label explosions. Follow-ups: observability-pack PRs will consume these templates and remove duplicated alert rules. This does not change any existing production alerts by itself; it only adds templates and examples.
changed_files: templates/performance/_shared.yaml (new: label allowlist, evaluation window defaults)
pkg/render/labels.go (enforce safe aggregation + optional region/tier enablement)
pkg/render/queries/model_latency.go (new query builder for p95 regression)
pkg/render/queries/model_throughput.go (new query builder for tokens/sec regression)
commits: Add ModelLatencyRegression + ModelThroughputRegression templates
Add shared perf template defaults (min samples, dampening, safe labels)
Add docs: per-model performance signals are SLO-like (not error budgets)
Add example perf-canary instantiation for phase 1 (us-east)
Add render/validate tests for templates and examples
review_conversation: Allison Grant: Please make sure we’re not encouraging region + model_version + prompt_shape + customer_id style labels. Can we explicitly document an allowlist and have validation fail if unknown labels are used?

Aisha Bello (author): Done. Added a strict label allowlist in pkg/render/labels.go and wired it into validate. Docs now call out that region/SKU are optional and should be enabled only when variance requires it.
Sean Gallagher: I like the min-sample + dampening defaults, but paging should require sustained regression (e.g., 30m) and ideally two consecutive windows. Do we have that pattern captured?

Aisha Bello (author): Added guidance + defaults: warn uses 10m eval window, page uses 30m with a 2-of-2 consecutive evaluation requirement (implemented as two sub-conditions with offset). Also updated docs with rationale (anti-flap).

Logan Wright: Tokens/sec query needs to be explicit about which metric we expect perf-canary to emit. Please reference the canonical metric name and include an aggregation example that won’t blow up cardinality.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated to use redwood_canary_tokens_per_sec (gaugehist summary) and included aggregation examples. Also added an explicit "required metrics" block in docs with links to ENG-4873.
Noah Patel: For latency regression, can we split runtime-only vs e2e? Scheduler queueing can dominate; having both will help triage.

Aisha Bello (author): Template now supports latency_scope = {e2e,runtime_only} via a required parameter; examples use e2e. Added note that runtime_only is recommended for kernel regressions and e2e for user impact and rollout gates.
Allison Grant: Looks good. Please add a CHANGELOG entry and ensure the examples pass in CI.
Aisha Bello (author): Added CHANGELOG.md entry; examples render in CI now.
Sean Gallagher: Approved once we confirm these templates are "not enabled" by default anywhere in prod.
Aisha Bello (author): Confirmed. No deployment wiring changes; only templates + docs + examples. Observability-pack will opt-in in a separate PR.
Allison Grant: Approved.
Sean Gallagher: Approved.
release_notes: New SLO Toolkit templates for per-model performance regressions (p95 latency and tokens/sec) intended for perf-canary alerting and rollout gates.
Adds docs and validated examples, plus guardrails to prevent unsafe metric label cardinality.

source.title: Add weekly status accuracy audit job (incident tags vs Statuspage updates)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Motivation: We’ve had recurring cases where the public status page didn’t reflect the impacted capability/region (e.g., streaming-only or embeddings-only degradations) even though the incident was tagged correctly internally, and vice-versa. This PR adds a lightweight weekly audit job to detect mismatches between (a) incident metadata (component_id/region/capability + severity + impact window) and (b) the Statuspage incident updates/components that were actually published. The goal is not to page or create more on-call work; it’s to produce a small, actionable report for follow-up during business hours and feed the status page transparency success metric (accuracy audits). What it does: 1) Runs weekly (Mon 09:00 PT) as a Kubernetes CronJob in the internal tooling cluster. 2) Pulls incidents from the incident timeline export endpoint (incident-bot export) for the prior 7 days, filtering to Sev0/Sev1 and any Sev2 that included a customer-facing status update flag. 3) Pulls corresponding Statuspage incidents/updates for the same time window using the internal Statuspage API client credentials (read-only). 4) Compares: (a) component_id list (including region/capability split), (b) first public update timestamp vs incident start (TTFU heuristic), and (c) whether the incident was marked resolved on Statuspage within 60 minutes of internal resolution. 5) Emits a report to #status-accuracy-audits (and #eng-sre on >N mismatches) with links to the incident, the Statuspage incident, and a short “likely fix” hint (e.g., missing region tag, component map mismatch, anti-flap prevented update, wrong capability selected). 6) Exposes Prometheus metrics for trend tracking (mismatch_count, ttfu_minutes_p50/p90, missing_component_tag_count) and adds a Grafana panel link in the output. Scope/Non-goals: This does not auto-edit Statuspage, does not create Linear tickets automatically (by design), and does not attempt to infer “true customer impact” from raw SLO data; it only checks consistency between internal incident tags and what we published. Safety/Noise controls: (a) Uses a allowlist of components that are currently GA on the public status page; anything behind rollout flags is reported as “skipped” to avoid false positives during rollout. (b) Has a minimum-duration threshold (default 5 min) before considering a mismatch relevant. (c) Dedupes repeated incidents due to Statuspage edits. (d) Stores the last audit cursor (time window) in a small ConfigMap to avoid double-reporting on reruns. Testing: Unit tests cover component normalization, time-window matching, and dedupe logic. Integration tests use recorded fixtures for both incident-bot export and Statuspage API responses. Rollout plan: Deploy to staging for 1 week with reports going to #status-accuracy-audits-staging, then enable prod schedule. Related: This supports the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and complements component mapping/schema work (ENG-4830) and incident-bot statuspage integration (ENG-4822).
commits: audit: add status accuracy auditor module + comparison logic
cronjob: wire weekly schedule + RBAC + config defaults
metrics: add prometheus counters/gauges + grafana panel link
tests: fixtures for incident export + statuspage API + normalization cases
docs: add runbook snippet and troubleshooting notes for audit failures
source.notable_snippet:
Example Slack output (trimmed):
- 2025-11-19 INC-1760048421 (Sev1): internal tags=[hosted.streaming/us-east] statuspage components=[hosted.api/us-east] => MISMATCH (capability)
  First public update: 23m after impact start (threshold: 15m)
  Links: incident timeline, statuspage incident, dashboard
  Hint: check component_id normalization in component map; see runbook section “Streaming vs API split”.
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): Love the direction. Two asks: (1) please make sure this never pages on-call; Slack-only is fine. (2) Can we include the statuspage incident id + component ids in the report so we can grep patterns?

Aisha Bello (author): Ack on both. No paging hooks added; only posts to #status-accuracy-audits with an optional secondary channel ping when mismatch_count > threshold. Added explicit statuspage_incident_id and component_id lists to the Slack block + JSON log line.
Allison Grant (review): Metrics naming: can we align labels with the public taxonomy schema? component_id/region/capability keys should match the registry to make dashboards consistent.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated metric labels to component_id, region, capability and added normalization via the same enum set used by slo-toolkit SLO labels. Added tests for a few edge cases (us-east vs us_east).
Christine Wong (review): The window matching logic worries me: statuspage updates can be edited later. How are we preventing double counting on reruns?

Aisha Bello (author): Added dedupe key = (incident_id, statuspage_incident_id, impact_start_rounded_5m). Also persists last_successful_window_end in a ConfigMap so reruns only re-check the most recent window. Edits still show up as a single row but with latest updated_at.
Miles Thornton (review): Please add a small runbook note for when the job itself fails (auth, API 429, etc). Also, rate limiting/backoff?

Aisha Bello (author): Added exponential backoff w/ jitter for Statuspage calls and hard cap (max 3 retries). Failures report a single ‘audit job degraded’ message to the channel with the error class + action, and emits audit_job_errors_total.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved after latest changes. This should help us quantify accuracy gaps without adding on-call load.
Allison Grant (approval): Approved. Thanks for aligning labels + adding fixtures.
release_notes: Internal: Adds a weekly status accuracy audit job comparing incident tags vs Statuspage component updates and reporting mismatches to #status-accuracy-audits.
Internal: Emits metrics for mismatch counts and TTFU distributions to support transparency initiative measurement.

What it does: 1) Runs weekly (Mon 09:00 PT) as a Kubernetes CronJob in the internal tooling cluster. 2) Pulls incidents from the incident timeline export endpoint (incident-bot export) for the prior 7 days, filtering to Sev0/Sev1 and any Sev2 that included a customer-facing status update flag. 3) Pulls corresponding Statuspage incidents/updates for the same time window using the internal Statuspage API client credentials (read-only). 4) Compares: (a) component_id list (including region/capability split), (b) first public update timestamp vs incident start (TTFU heuristic), and (c) whether the incident was marked resolved on Statuspage within 60 minutes of internal resolution. 5) Emits a report to #status-accuracy-audits (and #eng-sre on >N mismatches) with links to the incident, the Statuspage incident, and a short “likely fix” hint (e.g., missing region tag, component map mismatch, anti-flap prevented update, wrong capability selected). 6) Exposes Prometheus metrics for trend tracking (mismatch_count, ttfu_minutes_p50/p90, missing_component_tag_count) and adds a Grafana panel link in the output. Scope/Non-goals: This does not auto-edit Statuspage, does not create Linear tickets automatically (by design), and does not attempt to infer “true customer impact” from raw SLO data; it only checks consistency between internal incident tags and what we published. Safety/Noise controls: (a) Uses a allowlist of components that are currently GA on the public status page; anything behind rollout flags is reported as “skipped” to avoid false positives during rollout. (b) Has a minimum-duration threshold (default 5 min) before considering a mismatch relevant. (c) Dedupes repeated incidents due to Statuspage edits. (d) Stores the last audit cursor (time window) in a small ConfigMap to avoid double-reporting on reruns. Testing: Unit tests cover component normalization, time-window matching, and dedupe logic. Integration tests use recorded fixtures for both incident-bot export and Statuspage API responses. Rollout plan: Deploy to staging for 1 week with reports going to #status-accuracy-audits-staging, then enable prod schedule. Related: This supports the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and complements component mapping/schema work (ENG-4830) and incident-bot statuspage integration (ENG-4822).

source.title: Add component burn-rate alerts keyed by public status components (paging + runbook links)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we need component-level signals that are (a) consistent with our public component taxonomy and (b) actionable for on-call. This PR adds multi-window, multi-burn burn-rate alerts keyed by status component labels (component_id, region, capability) and routes pages to SRE on-call with direct links to the status page update runbook + per-component dashboards. Motivation: Recent incidents showed we were slow to declare partial impact (e.g., Streaming only, or us-east only) because our paging was still too service-oriented (gateway/scheduler) and didn’t map cleanly to the customer-facing component list. These alerts are intended to improve time-to-detection + reduce comms ambiguity by making the component/region impacted explicit from the first page. What’s included: 1) New alert rule groups for the public status components (Hosted API, Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console) with region scoping for us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast where metrics exist today; 2) Standard labels on alerts: component_id, region, capability, severity, and runbook; 3) Pager routing: page SRE on-call for Sev1/Sev0 burn (fast burn), and ticket/notify for slower burns; 4) Runbook links and dashboard annotations embedded into alert annotations, pointing to the status-page update runbook and component health dashboards; 5) Guardrails: basic anti-noise defaults (minimum traffic / request volume gate) to avoid paging on low-QPS components and staging. Thresholds: Uses standard multi-window burn-rate approach; initial defaults are tuned to mirror existing product SLO burn semantics but applied per-component. Fast burn: 5m/1h window, page. Slow burn: 30m/6h window, notify. Notes/assumptions: This PR assumes upstream instrumentation is (or will soon be) labeling request metrics with component_id/region/capability; where labels are missing today, the alert rules are present but disabled behind a feature flag until the observability pack lands. Rollout: Start with Hosted API + Streaming for us-east/us-west enabled; keep other components/regions disabled pending label coverage audit. Validation: Ran rules against last 7d in prod Prometheus; verified we would have paged correctly for the 2025-11-07 us-east streaming degradation without increasing page volume on normal days. Screenshots/links are in the internal thread referenced below.

source.title: Standardize SLO labels for public status components (component_id, region, capability)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Internal: SLO definitions now emit standardized labels (component_id, region, capability) aligned to status page taxonomy; includes a temporary compatibility alias for legacy dashboards/alerts.
source.description:
Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we’re mapping internal signals (SLO burn, error%, latency p95/p99) to customer-visible status components. The status page component taxonomy is keyed on (component_id, region, capability). Today our SLO definitions in slo-toolkit are inconsistent: some use `component`, others use `public_component`, `route_family`, or `region_name`, and capability is sometimes inferred from the SLI name. This makes it hard to (a) drive component-scoped burn alerts, (b) build consistent dashboards, and (c) correlate incidents/status updates with SLO impact.

This PR standardizes labels across all public-facing SLOs to emit:
- `component_id`: stable identifier matching the status component registry (e.g., `hosted_api`, `streaming`, `embeddings`, `reranking`, `console`)
- `region`: stable region key (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`) as used by the status page taxonomy (not cloud provider AZs)
- `capability`: one of `chat`, `streaming`, `embeddings`, `reranking`, `console` (kept intentionally small to avoid high-cardinality)

Implementation notes:
- Introduces a shared label-normalization helper so SLO specs don’t roll their own label sets.
- Updates affected SLO definitions to include the standardized label set and removes ad-hoc labels.
- Adds a temporary compatibility alias in recording rules so existing dashboards querying `component` continue to work during rollout (`component` is set to the value of `component_id`). The alias is gated behind a comment + TODO with a removal date after the Console + status page rollout completes.
- Updates validation to fail CI if any public SLO is missing any of the required labels.

Why now: This unblocks ENG-4841 (component-level burn alerts) and keeps the source-of-truth consistent with the status page component taxonomy work (ENG-4821/ENG-4830). It also reduces on-call confusion during incidents where we need to quickly determine which public component(s) are impacted.

Testing:
- `make test` (unit tests for label normalization)
- `make lint` (jsonnetfmt + promtool rule validation)
- `make render` (renders full ruleset and validates required label keys)

Rollout/compat:
- This is non-breaking for alert routing because alert labels are additive; however, downstream dashboards/alerts that query old labels may need updates. The compatibility alias should cover the most common case (`component`).
- Follow-up (separate PR): update dashboards/alerts to use `component_id` and remove alias after the staged rollout.

Screenshots/Examples:
- Example rendered alert labels now include `component_id=streaming`, `region=us-east`, `capability=streaming` for streaming SLO burn rules.

Refs:
- Status page component taxonomy + mapping work (internal) / status page transparency upgrades project.

Checklist:
- [x] Standardize labels across public SLO specs
- [x] Add CI guardrail for required labels
- [x] Provide short-term compatibility alias
- [x] Document label semantics and allowed values
- [x] Review with SRE/Observability stakeholders

Allison Grant: Looks good overall. Two questions: (1) can we constrain region values to match the status page taxonomy (no `us-east-1` etc)? (2) please add a guardrail so capability doesn’t accidentally become route-specific and explode cardinality.

Aisha Bello (author): Good call. I added an allowlist for `region` (`us-east|us-west|eu-west`) and `capability` is now an enum in the helper with a CI check.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on alias label for existing dashboards. Can we make it obvious this is temporary and put a removal target? Also, in the rendered rules, I still see `region_name` in two legacy SLOs.

Aisha Bello (author): Added TODO removal date + link to ENG-4841 follow-up to remove alias. Fixed the remaining `region_name` occurrences; render now only emits `region`.

Logan Wright: Minor: please update the naming to `component_id` everywhere (I saw one `componentId`). Also can we add a short snippet in docs explaining `component_id` vs `capability`?

Christine Wong: This aligns with the status component registry schema. Approved.

Final: All checks passed; approvals from Allison/Christine; Rafael approved after alias + legacy label fixes. Merged via squash.

source.title: Add overload error classification rules (separate from 5xx) for Hosted API SLO reporting
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes:
- SLO Toolkit now classifies overload/load-shed responses into a dedicated "overload" bucket (instead of generic 5xx).
- Adds policy-driven handling to optionally exclude overload from availability SLO burn while still tracking it as a first-class rate.
- Includes fixtures/tests for 429/503 overload payloads and header-based error signaling.
source.description:
Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding rollout, the gateway and downstream services now emit stable overload semantics (HTTP 429/503 + a Redwood error code + Retry-After). Our current SLO classifier treats most 5xx as availability failures, which makes overload-induced shedding look like an outage and obscures the intent of graceful degradation. This PR updates slo-toolkit classification rules so overload errors are tracked separately from generic 5xx while still being visible in dashboards/alerts.

What this changes: Adds a new classifier bucket `overload` and rules to map requests into it based on (1) the canonical Redwood error code (from `x-redwood-error-code` header or JSON body `error.code`) and (2) the overload HTTP status mapping (429/503 with overload code). Removes these responses from the default 5xx classifier so they no longer inflate `server_error` rates. Adds a policy switch to treat `overload` as excluded from the availability SLO calculation for Hosted API (per the updated SLO policy) while still emitting a parallel time series to quantify shed rate.

Signals supported: - Header: `x-redwood-error-code` in {`OVERLOAD_SHED`, `BREAKER_OPEN`, `CAPACITY_EXHAUSTED`, `DEPENDENCY_SATURATED`} - Body: `{"error":{"code":"overload_*"}}` (prefix match) - Status: 429/503 only count as overload when accompanied by an overload code (to avoid misclassifying generic 429 throttles)

Tests/validation: Adds fixtures using payloads from the gateway implementation PRs and validates (a) overload is not counted as 5xx, (b) availability burn excludes overload when `availability.exclude_overload=true`, (c) regression coverage for legacy 503s with no code still count as 5xx.

Rollout notes: This is a non-breaking change to the toolkit. Consumers should pull the latest rule bundle and confirm the new `overload` series is wired into dashboards. Follow-up work will update the default dashboard templates in observability-pack to include the new breakdown.

Refs: - Reliability program: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding - ADR-014 overload error taxonomy - Related PRs: redwood#18421, redwood#18472 - Linear: ENG-4826 / ENG-4822
commits: slo: add overload bucket and classification predicates
slo: support header + body error-code extraction (gateway format)
slo: add policy switch to exclude overload from availability burn
tests: fixtures for 429/503 overload responses + legacy 503 regression
docs: update classifier README with overload semantics
source.review_conversation:
Rafael Mendes: Can we ensure we only bucket 429 as overload when the overload error code is present? Otherwise we’ll swallow normal rate limits.
Aisha Bello: Yep — added an explicit guard: 429/503 are only overload if `x-redwood-error-code` is in the overload set OR body.error.code matches `overload_*`. A plain 429 still goes to `client_throttle`.

Allison Grant: Please document the new time series name and dimensions. Also, avoid high-cardinality labels (tenant_id) in the default rule output.
Aisha Bello: Updated README with the `http.errors.overload_rate` series and confirmed the rule output is keyed on service/route/region only. Tenant_id remains optional and off by default.

Christine Wong: What about legacy 503s during partial brownouts where no code is set? Those should still count as availability.
Aisha Bello: Added a regression test: 503 without code remains classified as `server_error`.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Add a note that excluding overload from availability is controlled by policy and should match the SLO doc.
Aisha Bello: Added to description + README and defaulted hosted policy to `exclude_overload=true` per current SLO guidance.

Approvals: Rafael Mendes approved. Allison Grant approved. Christine Wong approved.
source.post_merge_notes: Requested follow-up: update dashboard templates to display overload shed rate alongside availability (tracked in ENG-4826).

text:
Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding rollout, the gateway and downstream services now emit stable overload semantics (HTTP 429/503 + a Redwood error code + Retry-After). Our current SLO classifier treats most 5xx as availability failures, which makes overload-induced shedding look like an outage and obscures the intent of graceful degradation. This PR updates slo-toolkit classification rules so overload errors are tracked separately from generic 5xx while still being visible in dashboards/alerts.

What this changes: Adds a new classifier bucket `overload` and rules to map requests into it based on (1) the canonical Redwood error code (from `x-redwood-error-code` header or JSON body `error.code`) and (2) the overload HTTP status mapping (429/503 with overload code). Removes these responses from the default 5xx classifier so they no longer inflate `server_error` rates. Adds a policy switch to treat `overload` as excluded from the availability SLO calculation for Hosted API (per the updated SLO policy) while still emitting a parallel time series to quantify shed rate.

Signals supported: - Header: `x-redwood-error-code` in {`OVERLOAD_SHED`, `BREAKER_OPEN`, `CAPACITY_EXHAUSTED`, `DEPENDENCY_SATURATED`} - Body: `{"error":{"code":"overload_*"}}` (prefix match) - Status: 429/503 only count as overload when accompanied by an overload code (to avoid misclassifying generic 429 throttles)

Tests/validation: Adds fixtures using payloads from the gateway implementation PRs and validates (a) overload is not counted as 5xx, (b) availability burn excludes overload when `availability.exclude_overload=true`, (c) regression coverage for legacy 503s with no code still count as 5xx.

Rollout notes: This is a non-breaking change to the toolkit. Consumers should pull the latest rule bundle and confirm the new `overload` series is wired into dashboards. Follow-up work will update the default dashboard templates in observability-pack to include the new breakdown.
