source.author: Miles Thornton
source.body:
Title: Add time-to-ready SLI calculation (provisioning -> Ready) from event timestamps

Context
We’re rolling out provision-to-ready automation for Dedicated pools, and we need a canonical SLI for “time-to-ready” that is consistent across regions/GPU types and robust to retries/quarantine. This PR adds an SLI calculator in slo-toolkit that derives duration from the event stream produced by the provisioning orchestrator and fleet readiness service.

What this PR does
- Introduces a new SLI: time_to_ready_seconds (aka provision_to_ready_seconds) computed per node instance based on event timestamps.
- Uses event timestamps rather than scrape-time to avoid skew and to survive delayed ingestion.
- Defines the start and end markers as:
  - start: first Observed(Allocated) or ProvisioningStarted event for a node (prefers Allocated when present)
  - end: first Ready event (terminal Ready transition) for that node’s “bring-up attempt”
- Adds normalization logic to handle out-of-order events, duplicates, and multiple attempts.
- Adds guardrails so quarantined nodes do not silently poison the SLI distribution (we record as outcome=quarantined and exclude from success SLI, but still emit a separate failure duration).
- Adds unit tests and golden fixtures covering edge cases observed during baseline measurement.

SLI definition details (aligned with ENG-4821)
- SLI name: provision_to_ready_time
- Measure: (ready_ts - allocated_ts) in seconds
- Population: nodes in Dedicated and shared fleets that emit readiness events (pool_type tag)
- Success criteria: node reaches Ready within SLO window (SLO enforcement lives elsewhere; this PR only computes the raw duration distribution + outcomes)
- Outcome tags:
  - outcome=ready (terminal success)
  - outcome=quarantined (terminal failure)
  - outcome=unknown (insufficient data; excluded from SLI)

Implementation notes
- Added EventWindowResolver to group events into a single bring-up attempt. Rules:
  - Prefer the earliest allocated/provisioning_start timestamp as start.
  - If a node emits multiple allocated/provisioning_start events, treat them as retries within the same attempt unless a ReimageRequested or NodeReplaced event is present (then start a new attempt window).
  - If Ready occurs before start due to out-of-order ingestion, clamp by sorting on event_ts; if still inconsistent, mark unknown and emit a debug counter.
- Added dedupe by (node_id, event_type, event_ts) with a small tolerance window to avoid double-counting when the same state transition is re-emitted.
- Clock skew handling: if ready_ts - start_ts < -30s, mark outcome=unknown and increment provision_to_ready_clock_skew_total.

New metrics emitted by slo-toolkit
- slo_provision_to_ready_seconds (histogram; tags: region, gpu_type, pool_type, image_channel, outcome)
- slo_provision_to_ready_computed_total (counter; tags: outcome)
- slo_provision_to_ready_unknown_total (counter; tags: reason=missing_start|missing_ready|clock_skew|invalid_sequence)

Files changed (high level)
- pkg/slis/provision_to_ready.go: new SLI computation + windowing/dedupe
- pkg/slis/provision_to_ready_test.go: unit tests for edge cases
- pkg/events/schema.go: adds a small set of event types used to split attempts (ReimageRequested/NodeReplaced)
- pkg/export/prometheus.go: registers histogram + counters
- configs/slis.yaml: wires SLI into the default SLO bundle
- testdata/fixtures/provision_to_ready/*.jsonl: event stream fixtures from staged rollout simulations

Tests added / edge cases covered
- Missing Allocated but has ProvisioningStarted -> uses ProvisioningStarted as start.
- Duplicate Ready events -> only first terminal Ready ends the window.
- Out-of-order ingestion -> sorts by event_ts and validates sequence.
- Ready emitted, then Quarantined (should be impossible) -> treated as invalid_sequence (unknown) and flagged.
- Quarantined without Ready -> produces outcome=quarantined duration using quarantine_ts as terminal end.
- ReimageRequested mid-stream -> splits into a new attempt; only the successful attempt contributes to outcome=ready.

Rollout / compatibility
- Backwards compatible: no changes to existing SLIs.
- The new SLI is off by default unless included in the deployed slis.yaml bundle; this PR adds it to the default bundle in this repo, but production enablement is controlled in the deployment pipeline.

Follow-ups
- Once the control-plane readiness service is authoritative everywhere, we should remove fallback reliance on ProvisioningStarted for start_ts (kept for now to avoid gaps during staged rollout).
- Consider adding an explicit attempt_id emitted by the orchestrator; would simplify window splitting.

Release notes (internal)
- Adds time-to-ready SLI computation for provision-to-ready automation dashboards and alerting.

Links
- Linear: ENG-4821
- Related rollout work: provision-to-ready automation sprint

Checklist
- [x] Unit tests for resolver/windowing
- [x] Prometheus metric registration + naming review
- [x] Fixture coverage for out-of-order / duplicate events
- [x] Docs in configs/slis.yaml updated

PR conversation (abridged)
- Aisha Bello: “Can we ensure quarantined nodes don’t show up in the ‘success’ histogram? We need a separate view for failure duration.”
  - Miles Thornton: “Yes—added outcome tag and excluded outcome!=ready from success SLI aggregation; still emitting histogram with outcome label for analysis.”
- Allison Grant: “Please avoid high-cardinality labels (node_id). Also, image_version might be too granular vs image_channel.”
  - Miles Thornton: “Confirmed: no node_id labels. Switched to image_channel; image_version remains available via logs but not as a metric tag.”
- Rafael Mendes: “What’s our stance on clock skew? We saw some negative durations in baseline.”
  - Miles Thornton: “Added -30s tolerance; anything beyond that is unknown and increments a skew counter so we can quantify.”
- Sergio Costa: “Can we add a fixture for ‘Ready then Quarantined’ to ensure we detect invalid sequences?”
  - Miles Thornton: “Added invalid_sequence test and counter.”

CI
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- go-test-race: pass
- build: pass

Merge
Squash merged by Miles Thornton on 2025-10-14.

Add attempt window splitting (reimage/node replaced) and dedupe
Add fixtures + tests for out-of-order, duplicates, missing start
Wire SLI into default bundle and register Prometheus metrics
Polish: rename tags, add clock skew counter, docs/comments

source.title: Add incident timeline export (v1) for postmortems + incident-bot attachments
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes:
- New `incident_timeline_export_v1` JSON format with stable field names + versioning
- New CLI `slo-toolkit incident timeline export` to generate a timeline from annotations/events
- Adds validation + redaction hooks so exports are safe to attach in #incidents and postmortems
source.description:
Context
We keep repeating the same manual work during incidents + postmortems: someone screenshots dashboards, someone copies a deploy timeline, someone pastes 12 links, and we still don’t have a consistent machine-readable timeline that incident-bot (or postmortem tooling) can attach.

This PR adds a v1 incident timeline export format to slo-toolkit so that:
- incident-bot v2 can attach a lightweight timeline artifact (or deep link) in the first 5 minutes
- postmortem authors can import the same artifact into Confluence templates without reformatting
- we have a stable, versioned contract for future automation (e.g., “similar incident” matching)

What’s included
1) New export format: `incident_timeline_export_v1`
- Versioned: `schema_version: "1"`
- Ordered events with timestamps (RFC3339) and normalized types
- Designed to be tenant-safe by default: no raw request payloads, no secrets; all fields either allowlisted or redacted

2) CLI
- `slo-toolkit incident timeline export --incident-id <id> --window "-2h..+30m" --out timeline.json`
- Optional `--format json` (default) and `--pretty`
- Supports `--include` filters to cap output and reduce query cost (deploy, alerts, dashboards, notes)

3) Data sources (initial)
- Alert/annotation events from the existing SLO burn tooling annotation store
- Deploy events via a pluggable provider interface (so incident-bot/redwood control plane can wire in richer metadata later)
- Dashboard links via the existing burn-link generator (ties to ENG-5424 usage)

4) Guardrails
- Max events default = 250 (configurable)
- Truncation emits `truncated: true` and includes a summary note event
- Adds a lightweight validation step (`--validate`) used by CI

Example output (trimmed)
{
  "schema_version": "1",
  "incident_id": "inc_1739058123",
  "generated_at": "2025-02-25T19:06:12Z",
  "window_start": "2025-02-25T17:00:00Z",
  "window_end": "2025-02-25T20:00:00Z",
  "events": [
    {"ts":"2025-02-25T17:31:05Z","type":"alert_fired","title":"Hosted API 5xx burn","severity":"sev1","links":["https://console.redwoodinference.com/slo/burn?..." ]},
    {"ts":"2025-02-25T17:34:44Z","type":"deploy_started","title":"gateway rollout started","service":"api-gateway","region":"us-east-1","links":["https://console.redwoodinference.com/deploys/..." ]},
    {"ts":"2025-02-25T17:42:10Z","type":"mitigation","title":"Rollback initiated","notes":"Roll back gateway to image 7b1c2e9"}
  ]
}

Why this lives in slo-toolkit
- The format is closely tied to our SLO/incident analysis primitives: burn windows, annotation streams, and deep-link generation.
- Keeps incident-bot simpler: bot can call a single export endpoint / CLI wrapper instead of reimplementing timeline stitching.

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add first-class integration in incident-bot v2 context cards to attach this export (tracked in incident-bot workstream)
- Extend deploy provider to join PR links + canary results once deploy-metadata export is fully rolled out in redwood
- Add a compact "thread attachment" mode that outputs a Slack-friendly summary with deep links only

Testing
- Unit tests for schema validation, ordering, truncation, and filtering
- Golden test fixtures to prevent breaking changes
- Manual: generated export for a recent test incident and verified it imports into the postmortem template

Risk
Low runtime risk (tooling only). Primary risk is schema churn; mitigated by versioning + golden tests and explicit compatibility rules in docs.

Docs
- Adds `docs/incident-timeline-export.md` documenting contract, redaction, and migration rules.

Requested reviewers
SRE/incident automation + observability standards to ensure we’re not accidentally increasing telemetry cost or leaking sensitive info.
commits: Add incident timeline export schema + validator
Add timeline export CLI command + window parsing
Add event normalization and truncation behavior
Add deploy provider interface + stub implementation
Add golden tests and fixtures for timeline export v1
Docs: incident timeline export contract and compatibility notes
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Can we lock down which fields are allowed in `notes`? I don’t want responders pasting raw config or customer identifiers that then get exported.
Aisha Bello (author): Agreed. Added `notes_redaction` pass that (a) truncates to 512 chars (b) scrubs obvious secret patterns and emails (c) allows opt-out only behind `--unsafe-include-notes` which is not wired to incident-bot.

Miles Thornton: Can incident-bot call this without pulling a ton of telemetry during an outage? We’ve been burned by heavy queries (INT-3341).
Aisha Bello: This export intentionally uses annotation + deploy providers only; no high-cardinality metrics queries. Added max events, coalescing by type, and strict time window parsing; also added `--include` filters so bot can request a minimal set.

Logan Wright: Please ensure timestamps are always UTC and RFC3339; we’ve had parsing issues.
Aisha Bello: Updated parser and validator to reject non-RFC3339 and normalize to Z.

Rafael Mendes: What’s our compatibility policy? If postmortem tooling starts relying on this we can’t churn fields.
Aisha Bello: Added doc section: v1 is additive-only; removals/renames require bump to v2. Golden tests enforce stability.

Approval summary: 4 approvals after changes; no outstanding requests.

Context
We keep repeating the same manual work during incidents + postmortems: someone screenshots dashboards, someone copies a deploy timeline, someone pastes 12 links, and we still don’t have a consistent machine-readable timeline that incident-bot (or postmortem tooling) can attach.

This PR adds a v1 incident timeline export format to slo-toolkit so that:
- incident-bot v2 can attach a lightweight timeline artifact (or deep link) in the first 5 minutes
- postmortem authors can import the same artifact into Confluence templates without reformatting
- we have a stable, versioned contract for future automation (e.g., “similar incident” matching)

What’s included
1) New export format: `incident_timeline_export_v1`
- Versioned: `schema_version: "1"`
- Ordered events with timestamps (RFC3339) and normalized types
- Designed to be tenant-safe by default: no raw request payloads, no secrets; all fields either allowlisted or redacted

2) CLI
- `slo-toolkit incident timeline export --incident-id <id> --window "-2h..+30m" --out timeline.json`
- Optional `--format json` (default) and `--pretty`
- Supports `--include` filters to cap output and reduce query cost (deploy, alerts, dashboards, notes)

3) Data sources (initial)
- Alert/annotation events from the existing SLO burn tooling annotation store
- Deploy events via a pluggable provider interface (so incident-bot/redwood control plane can wire in richer metadata later)
- Dashboard links via the existing burn-link generator (ties to ENG-5424 usage)

4) Guardrails
- Max events default = 250 (configurable)
- Truncation emits `truncated: true` and includes a summary note event
- Adds a lightweight validation step (`--validate`) used by CI

source.title: Add error budget burn link generator (service/route/region)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Adds a small library + CLI for generating parameterized error budget burn URLs (service/route/region/time window) for embedding in incident-bot context cards and runbooks.
source.body:
Summary: This PR adds a link generator for Redwood SLO error budget burn dashboards. The intent is to standardize deep links used by incident-bot v2 and incident runbooks so responders land on the right service/route/region with a consistent time window and the correct burn-rate thresholds. Background: During recent incidents we had a mix of hand-rolled Grafana URLs (some missing route filters, some using the wrong dashboard UID, and a few with query params that broke on URL encoding). ENG-5424 calls for a single generator that the bot can use to produce links per region and route, with guardrails to avoid unbounded cardinality and to keep the URL stable even as dashboard internals evolve. What’s included: (1) A small TypeScript library `src/links/errorBudgetBurn.ts` that generates error budget burn URLs given `service`, optional `route`, optional `region`, optional `tenantTier` (for dashboard segmentation), and a `window` (last_15m/last_1h/last_6h/last_24h). (2) A canonical mapping of known environments and dashboard identifiers via `src/config/dashboards.ts` so call-sites don’t embed raw UIDs. (3) URL encoding utilities that explicitly escape route names like `/v1/chat/completions` and labels with `:` or `{}`. (4) A CLI `bin/slo-toolkit links:error-budget-burn` for quick manual generation while writing runbooks or triaging. (5) Tests that validate stable output formatting and correct encoding, plus snapshot coverage for common parameter combinations. Behavior/details: The generator supports three levels of specificity: service-only (global overview), service+region, and service+region+route. Route is passed as a label filter, not as a dashboard variable when possible, to avoid breaking when variable names change. Window defaults to `last_1h` (aligned with first-5-min incident context), with a `--window` override. We also include an optional `burnRatePreset` parameter that picks a standard pair of thresholds (e.g., 2h and 6h burn) so the same dashboard shows the same lines/annotations across teams. The generator avoids including tenant/customer identifiers entirely (not accepted as input), consistent with incident-bot constraints. Examples: `slo-toolkit links:error-budget-burn --service hosted-api --region us-east-1 --window last_1h` -> returns a URL scoped to the hosted-api dashboard with region filter and time range. `slo-toolkit links:error-budget-burn --service router --region eu-west-1 --route /v1/chat/completions --window last_15m` -> returns a URL with properly encoded route label filter. Integration notes: Incident-bot will call this generator (or re-implement via a thin wrapper) when building the “Error budget burn” card (ENG-5424). Runbooks should prefer the CLI output rather than copying raw Grafana URLs. This PR does not change any dashboards; it only generates links against existing UIDs and query params. Follow-ups: (a) Add a tiny wrapper in incident-bot repo for card rendering + fallback behavior when region/route is unknown (handled on bot side). (b) Document link parameter conventions in the incident context guidelines. Checklist: [x] Unit tests for encoding and stable output [x] CLI help + examples [x] No tenant identifiers supported [x] Works for hosted/dedicated/private env variants (prod/staging) [x] Reviewed with SRE/incident automation stakeholders. Risk: Low. Main risk is dashboard UID drift; we’ve centralized UIDs in `dashboards.ts` and added a single test that fails loudly if the UID set changes unexpectedly. If UIDs change intentionally, update the mapping and snapshots.

Commits: 1) feat(links): add error budget burn URL generator 2) feat(cli): add links:error-budget-burn command 3) test(links): snapshot coverage + encoding tests 4) chore(config): centralize dashboard UIDs and env base URLs

Changed areas (high level): - src/links/errorBudgetBurn.ts - src/config/dashboards.ts - src/cli/commands/linksErrorBudgetBurn.ts - src/links/__tests__/errorBudgetBurn.test.ts - README.md (CLI usage)

Review conversation (highlights): Allison Grant: Can we default to last_1h but allow bot to request last_15m for very fast-moving incidents? Also please confirm we don’t accept tenant IDs. Aisha Bello (author): Yes, default is last_1h; bot can override to last_15m. No tenant identifiers are accepted; only optional `tenantTier` (e.g., hosted/dedicated) which is not customer-specific. Miles Thornton: Incident-bot needs a deterministic link even when route is missing from context; can generator handle route undefined without producing broken filters? Aisha: Updated to omit the route filter entirely when route is undefined/empty. Added test coverage for blank route. Logan Wright: Please make sure route strings are encoded; we’ve had `/v1/*` routes break query params. Aisha: Added explicit encode for label values; added snapshot for `/v1/chat/completions` and a route with query-like characters. Chloe Martin: Dashboard UIDs are currently per-env; can we keep base URLs configurable? Aisha: Added env config and a `GRAFANA_BASE_URL` override for non-standard environments.

Approvals: Allison Grant approved. Miles Thornton approved. Logan Wright approved.

CI: build (pass), lint (pass), unit tests (pass). Merged via squash into main.

source.title: Add Runtime LTS SLO dashboards + rollout alerting
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Internal: Adds Grafana dashboards + Prometheus alert rules to monitor Runtime LTS traffic and patch rollouts (Hosted/Dedicated/Private where metrics are present).
source.body:
Context
Runtime LTS (runtime-1.18-lts) needs dedicated observability so we can (a) monitor error budget + latency specifically for LTS customer traffic, and (b) safely run patch rollouts with clear guardrails and fast detection of regressions. Existing dashboards aggregate across channels and mask LTS-specific signals, especially during patch week when canaries are running side-by-side with stable traffic.

This PR adds a small, opinionated LTS dashboard pack and alert rules to slo-toolkit. It is intended to be used by Release Eng + SRE during LTS patch releases and by on-call during steady-state to separate LTS from mainline noise.

What’s in this PR
1) Dashboards (Grafana)
- Runtime LTS Overview
  - Request rate (RPS), error rate, availability burn, and saturation signals scoped to LTS traffic
  - Latency heatmaps + p50/p95/p99 for LTS (by region, model family, and deployment mode when labels exist)
  - Top error reasons (HTTP status + Redwood error codes)
- Runtime LTS Patch Rollout
  - Split view for canary vs fleet using runtime_version + rollout_id
  - Delta panels: p95 latency delta vs baseline, error rate delta, throughput delta
  - “Rollback readiness” panels: crashloop, OOM, scheduler backpressure signals
- Runtime LTS Capacity & Queues
  - Queue depth/backpressure, shed rates, and batch sizing distribution (when present)
  - GPU utilization + KV cache pressure panels (best-effort depending on exporter availability)

2) Alerting (Prometheus rules)
- LTS Availability Burn
  - multi-window/multi-burn alerts scoped to runtime_channel="lts"
  - pages only at fast burn; slow burn is ticket/Slack
- LTS Patch Rollout Guardrails
  - Canary vs fleet regression alerts keyed off rollout_id (requires the rollout annotator in release workflow)
  - p95 latency regression alert during rollout window
  - 5xx / RedwoodError spike during rollout window
- LTS Crash / OOM / Restarts
  - container restarts above threshold for runtime pods with runtime_channel="lts"
  - GPU OOM rate elevated (if nvidia_dcgm exporter labels present)

3) Documentation + runbook pointers
- Adds a short README section describing required labels and how to wire rollout_id during patch release
- Adds alert annotations with links to existing on-call triage docs + the LTS patch runbook (Confluence link placeholder until the runbook is published externally)

Required / expected metric labels
- runtime_channel: "lts" | "main" (already emitted by gateway + runtime metrics adapter)
- runtime_version: e.g., "1.18.1-lts"
- rollout_id: unique ID set by release tooling during rollout (optional but required for rollout-specific alerts)
- deployment_mode: hosted | dedicated | private (best-effort; some Private deployments may not send this label)

Rollout plan
- Merge behind feature-flagged folder (dashboards are not auto-provisioned until we add them to the Grafana provisioning list in infra repo)
- SRE to enable dashboards for eng + on-call first
- Alerts default to “disabled” via alert routing label alert_scope="lts-pilot"; once pilot accounts start, routing will be turned on for the on-call rotation

Notes / limitations
- Private installs that don’t ship the standard metrics adapter will not get full fidelity (we still prefer to ship the dashboards; panels will show “no data” rather than misleading aggregates).
- For rollout regression alerts, rollout_id is mandatory. If not present, the dashboards still work (scoped by runtime_version), but guardrail alerts will not fire.

Screenshots
- Grafana: Runtime LTS Overview (link in internal comment)
- Grafana: Runtime LTS Patch Rollout (link in internal comment)

Risk
Low. This adds new dashboards/rules only and scopes queries to runtime_channel="lts". Existing SLOs are unchanged. Alert rules are initially routed to a non-paging destination until pilot cutover.

Test plan
- jsonnet compile for dashboards
- promtool rule validation
- Dry-run on staging Prometheus with synthetic LTS label series
- Verified queries do not match non-LTS traffic by inspecting label selectors

Related
- ENG-4821 Runtime LTS initiative epic
- ENG-4825 Integrate perf canary gates into LTS release (complements these runtime health guardrails)
- INT-3368 Benchmark runner capacity (separate but called out since patch-week load can impact SLO noise)
commits: slo/lts: add dashboard pack skeleton + folder structure
alerts: add lts availability burn (fast/slow) rules
dashboards: add lts patch rollout panels (canary vs fleet)
docs: add rollout_id wiring instructions + required label list
alerts: add restart/oom guardrails for lts channel
source.ci_summary: pass (grafana-jsonnet-compile, promtool-validate, unit, lint). One earlier failure on promtool-validate due to missing severity label; fixed in follow-up commit.
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant (comment): Can we make sure these queries don’t accidentally include mainline traffic? Some of our existing runtime metrics don’t always set runtime_channel.
Sergio Costa (reply): Good call. Updated selectors to require runtime_channel="lts" rather than regexp matching, and for a few exporters without the label we now explicitly exclude those series (panel shows no data). Also added a README note that missing labels will reduce fidelity rather than broaden scope.

Sean Gallagher (comment): For rollout guardrails, I want to avoid paging for a noisy p95 delta panel on small canaries. Do we have a minimum volume gate?
Sergio Costa (reply): Added a min-request-rate condition (over 5m) before evaluating regression thresholds. Also default route goes to alert_scope="lts-pilot" (non-paging) until we tune with pilot traffic.

Rafael Mendes (comment): Please add runbook links in annotations and make sure alert names include “LTS” so they’re searchable in PagerDuty.
Sergio Costa (reply): Done. Alert names are now RuntimeLTS* and annotations include triage steps + placeholder for the LTS patch runbook link.

Connor O'Brien (comment): Can we show runtime_version prominently on the rollout dashboard? Release managers will use this during patch week.
Sergio Costa (reply): Added a top-level variable + banner panel with runtime_version, rollout_id, and region.

Approvals: Sean Gallagher ✅, Allison Grant ✅, Rafael Mendes ✅, Connor O'Brien ✅

text:
Context
Runtime LTS (runtime-1.18-lts) needs dedicated observability so we can (a) monitor error budget + latency specifically for LTS customer traffic, and (b) safely run patch rollouts with clear guardrails and fast detection of regressions. Existing dashboards aggregate across channels and mask LTS-specific signals, especially during patch week when canaries are running side-by-side with stable traffic.

This PR adds a small, opinionated LTS dashboard pack and alert rules to slo-toolkit. It is intended to be used by Release Eng + SRE during LTS patch releases and by on-call during steady-state to separate LTS from mainline noise.

What’s in this PR
1) Dashboards (Grafana)
- Runtime LTS Overview
  - Request rate (RPS), error rate, availability burn, and saturation signals scoped to LTS traffic
  - Latency heatmaps + p50/p95/p99 for LTS (by region, model family, and deployment mode when labels exist)
  - Top error reasons (HTTP status + Redwood error codes)
- Runtime LTS Patch Rollout
  - Split view for canary vs fleet using runtime_version + rollout_id
  - Delta panels: p95 latency delta vs baseline, error rate delta, throughput delta
  - “Rollback readiness” panels: crashloop, OOM, scheduler backpressure signals
- Runtime LTS Capacity & Queues
  - Queue depth/backpressure, shed rates, and batch sizing distribution (when present)
  - GPU utilization + KV cache pressure panels (best-effort depending on exporter availability)

2) Alerting (Prometheus rules)
- LTS Availability Burn
  - multi-window/multi-burn alerts scoped to runtime_channel="lts"
  - pages only at fast burn; slow burn is ticket/Slack
- LTS Patch Rollout Guardrails
  - Canary vs fleet regression alerts keyed off rollout_id (requires the rollout annotator in release workflow)
  - p95 latency regression alert during rollout window
  - 5xx / RedwoodError spike during rollout window
- LTS Crash / OOM / Restarts
  - container restarts above threshold for runtime pods with runtime_channel="lts"
  - GPU OOM rate elevated (if nvidia_dcgm exporter labels present)

3) Documentation + runbook pointers
- Adds a short README section describing required labels and how to wire rollout_id during patch release
- Adds alert annotations with links to existing on-call triage docs + the LTS patch runbook (Confluence link placeholder until the runbook is published externally)

Required / expected metric labels
- runtime_channel: "lts" | "main" (already emitted by gateway + runtime metrics adapter)
- runtime_version: e.g., "1.18.1-lts"
- rollout_id: unique ID set by release tooling during rollout (optional but required for rollout-specific alerts)
- deployment_mode: hosted | dedicated | private (best-effort; some Private deployments may not send this label)

- Merge behind feature-flagged folder (dashboards are not auto-provisioned until we add them to the Grafana provisioning list in infra repo)

- Private installs that don’t ship the standard metrics adapter will not get full fidelity (we still prefer to ship the dashboards; panels will show “no data” rather than misleading aggregates).
- For rollout regression alerts, rollout_id is mandatory. If not present, the dashboards still work (scoped by runtime_version), but guardrail alerts will not fire.

- Grafana: Runtime LTS Patch Rollout (link in internal comment)

source.title: Burn-rate alerts for Dedicated throughput SLO (recommended thresholds + templates)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description:
Context: As part of Dedicated throughput guarantees, we need consistent burn-rate alerting across regions/customers that matches the SLO semantics defined in the throughput SLO evaluator (PR #311). Today each service/team has been hand-rolling rule expressions and thresholds (with mismatched windows and inconsistent annotations), which is creating alert noise and complicating incident triage.

This PR adds burn-rate alert generation helpers specifically for the Dedicated Throughput SLO signal ("throughput floor met" / "below floor"), plus recommended default thresholds and alert templates (labels/annotations/runbook links). The goal is to make it trivial for downstream repos (observability-pack, infra-config) to generate standard multi-window burn-rate alerts and keep them aligned with SLO semantics.

What’s included: (1) New helper: generate_burnrate_alerts_for_throughput_slo(...) that wraps the generic burn-rate generator with Dedicated-specific defaults (page/ticket thresholds, window pairs, labels). (2) Recommended thresholds tuned for Dedicated throughput SLO: Paging on fast burn (e.g., 2h budget consumed in ~1h) and ticket on slow burn (e.g., 2d budget consumed in ~1d), using the standard multi-window approach. (3) Alert rule templates with consistent annotations: deployment_id/customer/tier/model/region, dashboard URL, and runbook URL. (4) Documentation and examples showing how to wire the helper into rule generation pipelines.

Notes/assumptions: The helper expects an SLO record exported by the throughput SLO evaluator with a boolean-like compliance signal over time and/or an error_ratio series (below-floor time / total time), keyed by {deployment_id, region, model, tier}. This PR does not change the underlying SLO computation; it only standardizes alert generation. The default windows are chosen to balance detection speed vs noise for throughput variability (streaming + batching) and match the oncall policy agreed in ENG-19288.

Out of scope: (a) owning the downstream rollout of these alerts into prod rule groups (that will happen in observability-pack / infra repos), (b) changing the Dedicated throughput SLO definition, and (c) credit/remedy logic.

Testing: Unit tests cover rule rendering, label propagation, and threshold/window configuration. Added a golden-file style snapshot test to avoid drift in template strings.

Follow-ups: (1) Wire these helpers into observability-pack rule groups for Dedicated and run a 1-week shadow period to measure noise before paging. (2) Add optional per-tier override knobs (Premium stricter paging) once tier taxonomy stabilizes.
commits: slo/alerts: add throughput-specific burn-rate helper
slo/alerts: add recommended window pairs + default thresholds
templates: standardize annotations (dashboard/runbook links)
tests: golden snapshot for rendered rules
docs: add example usage for Dedicated throughput SLO alerts
conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): The direction is right. Two questions: (1) can we make the window pairs match our standard burn-rate policy (short=30m/2h, long=6h/1d), and (2) please ensure the annotations include the SLO name/id so we can correlate in reports.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated defaults to use the standard multi-window pairs we discussed in ENG-19288 (30m/2h for page, 6h/1d for ticket), and added slo_id + slo_name into annotations. Also ensured labels include service="dedicated" for routing.

Rafael Mendes (review): Please make sure the helper doesn’t assume PromQL-specific syntax in the string templates since some consumers render to Mimir rule groups and others to Grafana-managed alerts. Keep the expressions separate from the annotations. Also: add runbook_url as a first-class field, not embedded in description.

Aisha Bello (author): Good call—refactored so the helper returns a structured AlertRule object (expression + labels + annotations) but still serializes cleanly for Prometheus/Mimir. runbook_url is now a dedicated annotation key with a stable path.

Allison Grant (review): Can we include a canonical dashboard link format and ensure it’s URL-safe (deployment_id can have dashes/UUIDs)? Also, I’d like the templates to include tier to make it easier for CS/SRE triage.

Aisha Bello (author): Added URL encoding for variables in the dashboard URL, and included tier in both labels + annotations. Dashboard URL matches the dedicated-throughput-slo dashboard spec format (region, deployment_id, model, tier).
Sergio Costa (review): Snapshot test is great. One tweak: please add an example snippet in docs showing how to override thresholds for a specific deployment (we’ll need this during beta).
Aisha Bello (author): Added a short override example in docs/alerts/throughput_slo_burnrate.md (per-deployment config block).
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved. Defaults and templates now match SRE guidance. Please file a follow-up for tier-specific paging policy once Premium semantics are locked.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. Thanks for keeping expressions/tooling-agnostic.
release_notes: Added Dedicated throughput SLO burn-rate alert helper with recommended default windows and thresholds.
Standardized alert labels/annotations (deployment_id, tier, model, region) + runbook/dashboard links.

source.title: Add SLOs for Private backup job success + restore drill latency (with burn-rate alerts)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Introduce SLO definitions + burn-rate alerting hooks for Private Day-2 backup job success and automated restore drill latency; adds standard labels/suppression guidance for maintenance windows.
source.description: Title: Add SLOs for Private backup job success + restore drill latency (with burn-rate alerts) Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore + DR readiness work, we need first-class SLOs that can be consumed by SRE + on-call automation (and mirrored into customer-facing dashboards where applicable). This PR adds SLO definitions and standard burn-rate alerting hooks for: (1) backup job success rate and (2) automated restore drill latency. These SLOs are intended to be used by Redwood SRE for Redwood-managed Private environments and as reference defaults for customer deployments running our observability pack. Goals: - Provide consistent SLI/SLO definitions with clear scopes/labels - Wire standard multi-window burn-rate alerts (fast + slow) - Avoid noisy paging for non-prod while preserving signal for prod/private - Document assumptions + required metrics emitting behavior Dependencies/related work: - Metrics emission: redwood PR-24344 (structured metrics around backup/restore) - Dashboards/alerts: observability-pack PR-94 (visualization + alert routing) - Backup/restore automation: redwood-private-installer PR-482 / PR-489; helm chart CronJob PR-131; restore drill automation ENG-9827 SLOs included: 1) Backup job success (Private control plane) - SLI: error ratio over job runs, measured from counter redwood_backup_job_runs_total{env="prod",deployment="private",component="control-plane",status=~"success|failure"} - Objective: 99.5% successful backup runs over a 28d rolling window (allows for transient storage/KMS issues while still pushing us to harden) - Notes: This is a job-based SLO (not request-based). We explicitly exclude manual test runs and include scheduled CronJob runs only via label job_type="scheduled". We also include a short grace for deployments during maintenance windows via a documented suppression label. 2) Restore drill latency (Private staging + periodic prod drill) - SLI: proportion of drills completing within threshold AND latency distribution; the SLO uses a latency-based SLI derived from redwood_restore_drill_duration_seconds_bucket and redwood_restore_drill_runs_total. - Objective: 95% of restore drills complete in <= 45 minutes over a 28d rolling window - Secondary indicator (non-SLO but charted): p95 restore drill duration in seconds over the last 7d for operational tuning - Notes: By default, restore drills are expected to run in staging (to validate tooling without impacting customers). For prod/private environments where customers opt in to periodic drills, we use the same SLO definition but default alert routing is “ticket” unless deployment label enable_paging="true" is set. Alerting strategy: - Burn-rate alerts for backup success: - Fast burn: 2h / 6h windows, page if burning > 14x (aligned with other job SLOs) - Slow burn: 1d / 3d windows, page if burning > 3x - Burn-rate alerts for restore drill latency: - Ticket on slow burn by default (staging), page only when enable_paging="true" - Includes explicit “no data” handling: if no drill has run in the expected schedule window, we emit a separate warning alert (restore_drill_missing) to catch cron/scheduler failures. What changed: - Added new SLO spec files under slo/private/ for: backup_job_success and restore_drill_latency - Added reusable recording rules for job-based error ratio and restore drill latency SLI - Added burn-rate alert templates + routing annotations (severity, runbook, owner) - Updated README with label requirements, maintenance window suppression guidance, and example PromQL queries Files (high level): - slo/private/backup_job_success.yaml - slo/private/restore_drill_latency.yaml - recording_rules/private_backup_restore.rules.yaml - alerts/private_backup_restore_burnrate.yaml - alerts/private_restore_drill_missing.yaml - README.md (Private Day-2 section) - tests/promql_lint_test.go (adds fixtures for new rules) - fixtures/prometheus_rules_expected.txt (updated golden) Example PromQL snippets: - Backup success rate (28d): sum(rate(redwood_backup_job_runs_total{env="prod",deployment="private",component="control-plane",status="success",job_type="scheduled"}[28d])) / sum(rate(redwood_backup_job_runs_total{env="prod",deployment="private",component="control-plane",job_type="scheduled"}[28d])) - Restore drill <=45m (ratio): sum(rate(redwood_restore_drill_runs_total{env="prod",deployment="private",result="success",duration_le="2700"}[28d])) / sum(rate(redwood_restore_drill_runs_total{env="prod",deployment="private"}[28d])) Rollout notes: - These SLO configs are safe to ship and consume immediately, but require the metrics from redwood PR-24344 (or equivalent) to avoid “no data” conditions. README calls this out explicitly. - For existing environments with legacy metric names, we intentionally did not add compatibility shims in slo-toolkit; compatibility mapping should live in the metrics emission layer or the observability pack. Testing: - promtool check rules (CI) - Unit tests for rule generation + golden fixtures - Manual validation against staging Prometheus with synthetic series (see README commands) Checklist: - [x] SLO definitions reviewed with SRE (Sean/Neha) - [x] Alert severity + routing aligns with on-call policy (no default paging for staging) - [x] Runbook links added (placeholders pointing to Confluence until public docs are live) - [x] Label taxonomy documented (env/deployment/component/job_type/enable_paging) - [x] CI green
commits: slo(private): add backup job success SLO scaffold + recording rules
slo(private): add restore drill latency SLO + missing-drill alert
alerts: add multi-window burn-rate alerts w/ routing annotations
docs: document label requirements + maintenance window suppression
tests: update golden fixtures for new recording rules
source.review_conversation: Allison Grant (Observability): The label taxonomy looks right, but can we ensure the SLO scope is strictly Private control plane? I’m worried we’ll accidentally include dedicated/hosted metrics if labels drift. Also, please add explicit annotations for owner + runbook so downstream tooling can route correctly. Sergio Costa (author): Good call. I tightened matchers to deployment="private" and component="control-plane" for the backup SLO and added requiredLabels docs. Added owner=eng-sre and runbook annotations in both alert groups. Sean Gallagher (SRE): Burn-rate thresholds: 14x fast burn is fine for paging, but restore drill alerts shouldn’t ever page by default. We’ve had staging drills flap due to resource pressure. Can we make paging opt-in? Sergio Costa: Updated restore drill burn-rate alerts to severity=warning + route=ticket by default, and added enable_paging="true" gate for paging. Also added restore_drill_missing as warning to catch schedule failures without waking people up. Neha Kapoor (Reliability): For backup jobs, please add a small “for:” to avoid paging on a single scrape gap or one-off job failure. Also, what’s the plan for maintenance windows? Sergio Costa: Added for: 15m to fast burn and for: 1h to slow burn. Documented maintenance suppression via alertmanager silence + optional label maintenance_window="true" propagated by the env overlay (README). Rafael Mendes (Game day owner): The 45m threshold is OK for reference deployments, but we should call out that on-prem NFS backends may be slower. Ensure the doc states this is a default and can be tuned. Sergio Costa: Added README note about tuning restore threshold per environment and recommended baselining during first game day. Allison Grant: Approved after tightening matchers and adding annotations. Sean Gallagher: Approved with opt-in paging change. Neha Kapoor: Approved.

Alerting strategy: - Burn-rate alerts for backup success: - Fast burn: 2h / 6h windows, page if burning > 14x (aligned with other job SLOs) - Slow burn: 1d / 3d windows, page if burning > 3x - Burn-rate alerts for restore drill latency: - Ticket on slow burn by default (staging), page only when enable_paging="true" - Includes explicit “no data” handling: if no drill has run in the expected schedule window, we emit a separate warning alert (restore_drill_missing) to catch cron/scheduler failures.
